footer.tagline: Reskilling & promoting education for refugees, creating quality job opportunities and bringing them into the global community.

hero.subtitle: Generation Aid equips youth in Kakuma refugee camp with digital skills, entrepreneurship training, and pathways to employment — so they can shape their own future.

about.title: A youth refugee-led organisation
about.body1: Generation Aid is a youth Refugee-Led Organisation (RLO) born in 2019 in Kakuma, transforming education-into-employment systems so refugees can integrate into the global economy.
about.body2: We are built, led and delivered by refugees themselves — designing programs that match the realities of camp life and the opportunities of the global digital economy.
about.visionBody: Empowering neglected refugees with innovative programs that reduce unemployment and increase income — so they can become active members of the global community and economy.
about.missionBody: To transform refugees' lives by providing vocational skills and EdTech programs that create educational and employment pathways.

amani.quote: Generation Aid gave me my first remote contract. Today I support my family by working online from Kakuma — something I never thought possible.

mwangi.quote: The Senga Gallery changed everything. For the first time, my paintings are seen — and they tell the story of where I come from.

hero.title: Who are we?
hero.subtitle: Generation Aid is a youth Refugee-Led Organisation (RLO) dedicated to transforming lives through vocational skills, EdTech, education, livelihood and entrepreneurship programs — creating educational and employment pathways for refugee communities.

intro.p1: We are transforming education-into-employment systems — preparing, placing and supporting people into life-changing careers that would otherwise be inaccessible — so refugees can integrate into the global economy.
intro.p2: Generation Aid is a youth-led refugee nonprofit based in Kenya's Kakuma Refugee Camp, born in 2019 during COVID-19. Over the years, GAID has worked tirelessly to provide a range of services to displaced communities — vocational education, entrepreneurship, food distribution and digital skills.
intro.p3Suffix: to reflect our ambition of serving refugees and displaced people in this current generation.

about.objectivesSubtitle: What Generation Aid works to achieve.
objectives: Empower refugees with knowledge and skills — education, digital skills, livelihood and entrepreneurship — so they can earn a sustainable income through remote work opportunities.
Equip refugees with the essential knowledge and competencies to navigate the digital world, fostering creativity and problem-solving.
Build sustainable community development projects through long-term initiatives in education, entrepreneurship and social support.

focusedPrograms.title: Vocational & Livelihood skills
focusedPrograms.body: Vocational training, small-business support and income-generating projects that help refugees build sustainable, self-reliant futures.

focusedPrograms.title: Education
focusedPrograms.body: Learning tools and inclusive programs that support education for all — especially girls and children with disabilities — and bridge youth into higher education.

focusedPrograms.title: EdTech
focusedPrograms.body: Digital learning tools, inclusive education and skills training that close the education gap in displacement settings.

focusedPrograms.title: Entrepreneurship
focusedPrograms.body: Training, resources and market access that help refugees launch sustainable businesses and achieve self-reliance.

focusedPrograms.title: Remote Employment
focusedPrograms.body: Digital-skills training plus our Digital Community Hub — high-speed internet, stable power and devices — connecting refugees to global remote work.

values.title: Refugee-led, community-driven
values.body: We work as a group of passionate refugees and make sure youth voices in the community are heard.

values.title: Accountability & Transparency
values.body: We are answerable for our actions and conduct every activity with full transparency.

values.title: Self-reliance
values.body: We build self-sustaining pathways so the organisation can keep supporting the community even without external support.

history.p1Start: Generation Aid was born in 2019 from the vision, determination and lived experience of our founder
history.p1End: — a social entrepreneur and changemaker on a mission to transform the lives of refugees through digital skills, innovative education and economic empowerment.
history.p2Start: As a Congolese refugee who has lived in Kakuma refugee camp for over nine years, Hubert has witnessed firsthand the immense barriers refugees face: limited access to education, unemployment and the struggle for dignity. But rather than accept these challenges as fate, he chose to
history.p3Start: GAID's work is grounded in the belief that refugees and other displaced individuals have the right to live with dignity and respect, and should have access to the resources and opportunities they need to rebuild their lives. We became a Community Based Organisation in 2019, transitioned to the name
history.p3End: the same year, and received our Government certificate in 2025.
history.p4: Through Generation Aid we are not just closing literacy and unemployment gaps — we are building a future where every refugee has the tools, knowledge and opportunities to shape their destiny. This is more than a movement; it is a revolution of hope, innovation and unstoppable resilience.
history.p5: We are grateful to our partners and donors who have been instrumental in this journey of empowering youth and advancing the UN Strategic Development Goals 2030 of Quality Education, Reduced Inequality, Decent Work and Economic Growth.

about.teamSubtitle: A refugee-led team of passionate, transparent and accountable changemakers.
about.boardSubtitle: Our board provides strategic direction, ensures accountability, and supports Generation Aid as we grow.

chair.name: Board Chair
chair.bio: Provides strategic leadership for the organisation and chairs the board of directors.

treasurer.name: Treasurer
treasurer.bio: Oversees financial stewardship, audits and transparent reporting to partners and donors.

secretary.name: Board Secretary
secretary.bio: Maintains board records, governance policies and ensures compliance with statutory obligations.

about.getInvolvedSubtitle: There are many ways to stand with us — choose what fits you best.

hero.title: Education & Capacity Building
hero.subtitle: Vocational and education programs in ICT, tailoring & dressmaking craft training, English language skills, and other innovative education that plays a critical role in the economic and livelihood development of refugee youth in the Kakuma refugee camp.

youthDigital.title: From the camp to the global digital economy
youthDigital.subtitle: Equipping refugee youth with the skills, tools and connections to earn online — closing the digital divide one trainee at a time.

creativity.title: Art crossing borders
creativity.subtitle: A single canvas sparked a movement — bringing materials, workshops and dignity to refugee artists in Kakuma Camp.

cta.title: Help us reach more refugee youth.
cta.subtitle: Sponsor a program, donate equipment or join us as a volunteer.

overview.title: Computer Literacy Skills
overview.body: ICT and digital literacy training that prepares refugee youth for both online and offline careers.

overview.title: Tailoring & Dressmaking
overview.body: Hands-on craft training paired with entrepreneurship coaching so learners can launch their own tailoring businesses.

overview.title: English Language Skills
overview.body: Communicative English classes for beginner, intermediate and advanced learners — built around real daily interactions.

details.title: Computer Literacy Skills
details.body: Information and Communication Technologies (ICT) are essential skills for any career a student may want to pursue. At GAID we offer two ICT courses tailored to the needs of youth in Kakuma — Basic ICT & Digital Literacy and an introduction to programming. With these skills we unlock the creativity of youth and enable them to transform their communities through innovation and virtual entrepreneurship, paving the way for decent livelihoods and economic growth both online and offline.

details.title: Tailoring & Dressmaking Craft Training
details.body: Our craft training program provides practical tailoring and dressmaking skills through hands-on training and attachments with local tailoring businesses. Participants also take part in entrepreneurship training to equip them with the skills needed to start and run their own successful businesses — creating their own opportunities and achieving self-sufficiency.

details.title: English Language Skills
details.body: GAID's English language program is designed for beginners, intermediates and advanced learners. We offer Communicative English classes for youth who need to improve day-to-day interactions and return to their communities with a better understanding of English in both written and oral forms.

bpo.title: Remote Work through the BPO Model
bpo.p1: One of Generation Aid's flagship initiatives is the Remote Work Training & Freelancing Bootcamp, designed to equip refugee youth with the digital skills needed to access online job opportunities. In Kakuma, where traditional employment is scarce, remote work offers a lifeline to financial independence. The program trains participants in graphic design, digital marketing, content writing and virtual assistance — giving them marketable skills for a global market and connecting them to remote work opportunities.
bpo.p2: The bootcamp includes hands-on workshops, mentorship from experienced freelancers, and dedicated workstations with internet access so participants can practise their skills and apply for jobs. By the end of the training, many graduates secure remote contracts — enabling them to earn a sustainable income, support their families and reduce reliance on humanitarian aid.

coding.eyebrow: 2. Building the next generation of developers
coding.title: Coding & Web Development Academy
coding.p1: Beyond technical training, participants work on real-world projects — building e-commerce websites and digital storytelling platforms for refugee artists. Hackathons and coding challenges encourage innovation by tasking students with solving local problems through technology.
coding.p2: The program also connects aspiring developers with career guidance, scholarships and internship opportunities — providing a clear pathway into the global tech industry.
coding.p3: These initiatives reflect Generation Aid's commitment to bridging the digital divide and ensuring that refugee youth are not left behind in the digital economy.

kap.howP1: Boat — the painting that started all of this — led Tara Dominick and Hubert into a conversation about the existence of art in the Kakuma Refugee Camp. What followed was the founding of the Kakuma Art Project (KAP).
kap.howP2: Once committed to the project, it was as if everything started to align. Tara initially offered two hours a week, but the project now occupies three or four days of her week — and her thoughts constantly.
kap.howP3: From conception, Tara sold 10 limited edition signed prints of Boat to fund Phase 1 — getting paints, brushes, palette knives, paper, charcoal and canvasses into the camp.
kap.quote: One small action can ripple out to create significant change.
kap.quoteAttr: — Bob Brimson, one of the first believers in the project. He coined the tagline “the power of one painting”.
kap.beautyBody: The project provides artists at the camp with education, visibility and opportunity — supported by a continuing series of workshops run through the Generation Aid Centre in Kakuma Camp.
kap.paintingP1: Mwangi, a 52-year-old artist living at the Kakuma Camp, creates beautiful images of his homeland. Yet for years he was limited by resources — no canvas, no paint, no brushes.
kap.paintingP2: His passion to paint meant salvaging old t-shirts from the dump, washing them, and coating them with glue to create a makeshift canvas. His ingenuity and passion moved Tara deeply when she first saw his work.
kap.paintingP3: His work has given the root of the program and is now well recognised.
kap.activitiesP1: Many refugees in Kakuma come from rich cultural backgrounds with strong artistic traditions in beading, weaving, tailoring and wood carving. However, limited market access prevents them from turning these skills into sustainable businesses. To address this, Generation Aid established the Refugee Artisan & Handicraft Cooperative — a platform that supports refugee artisans in producing and selling their handmade products to local and international markets.
kap.activitiesP2: The initiative provides specialised training in various handicrafts, helping artisans improve product quality, develop unique designs and scale their production. It also connects them with buyers, NGOs and online marketplaces where they can showcase and sell their work. Through storytelling and branding workshops, artisans learn how to share their journey and cultural heritage — making their products more valuable in global markets.
kap.celebratingTitle: A cooperative model that pools strength
kap.celebratingP1: To further support artisans, Generation Aid is developing a cooperative model that lets artisans pool resources, access small business loans and collectively invest in materials and equipment. This ensures that even those with limited financial means can participate and grow their businesses.
kap.celebratingP2: The Refugee Artisan & Handicraft Cooperative not only preserves cultural heritage but also creates sustainable jobs and economic opportunities for artisans — particularly women and youth. By giving them a platform to monetise their skills, this initiative empowers refugees to build independent and dignified livelihoods beyond humanitarian aid.
kap.galleryTitle: More than a building — an art gallery at the heart of Kakuma
kap.galleryP1: The first ever art gallery has been built — becoming a beacon of hope, a place for refugees to express themselves, to inspire one another, and to stand proud alongside each other as they move forward with their lives.
kap.galleryP2: KAP is run by Tara Dominick, a fine artist from Devon, together with Hubert Senga and Generation Aid who manage delivery of the art programmes on the ground in Kakuma.
kap.galleryCaption1: The workshop organised by the Birgheter project, to promote beauty and pride for the refugees.
kap.galleryCaption2: Through running art workshops, Generation Aid learns the real challenges the community is facing and develops community-based solutions.
kap.galleryP3: Through running workshops the refugee community in Kakuma comes together to advocate for peace, unity and talent. It is a unique program giving refugees a platform to share what's hidden in their cultures — gifts the wider world can learn from.
kap.galleryCaption3: The first ever art gallery in Kakuma refugee camp — named The Senga Gallery to honour the refugee advocacy of Hubert Senga, founder of Generation Aid.
kap.whySubtitle: The gallery is inclusive — built to promote creativity among refugees.

whyCards.title: Expression & Identity
whyCards.body: A safe space where refugee artists can give form to memories of home, stories of journey, and visions of tomorrow.

whyCards.title: Solar-Powered Gallery
whyCards.body: Solar panels ensure there's always light to see the artwork — making the gallery viable and sustainable long-term.

whyCards.title: Community & Solidarity
whyCards.body: Artists stand alongside one another, building pride and connection within a community that has lost so much.

whyCards.title: Global Visibility
whyCards.body: Through exhibitions like the one in Wiltshire, refugee stories reach new audiences — bridging worlds through art.

hero.subtitle: We measure what matters — opportunities created, livelihoods restored, and futures unlocked. Every number below represents a person whose potential is finally being met.

blog.haveAStory: Have a story? Get in touch
blog.aboutUsBody: Over the years, GAID has worked tirelessly to provide a range of services to displaced communities — including entrepreneurship, vocational education and digital skills. We became a legal Community-Based Organisation in 2019 and later transitioned to the name Generation Aid, before receiving our Government certificate in 2025. The name reflects our ambition of serving refugees and displaced youth within the Kakuma refugee camp.
blog.momentsSubtitle: Photos uploaded by our team — workshops, graduations, daily life in the Kakuma hub.
blog.postNotFoundSubtitle: The article you're looking for doesn't exist.

hero.title: We'd love to hear from you
hero.subtitle: Questions, partnerships, press, volunteering — drop us a line and we'll get back to you within a few working days.
hero.subtitleAlt: Whether you want to partner, donate, volunteer or just say hello — we read every message and reply within two business days.

contact.allFieldsRequired: All fields required unless marked optional.

donate.a: Use the Donate button anywhere on the site — it links to our verified GlobalGiving page. For larger gifts or corporate matching, email info@generationaid.org and we'll arrange a direct transfer or invoice.

volunteer.a: Yes. We work with remote mentors in design, writing, software, English language and career coaching. Send us a note with your skills and weekly availability via the form above.

spend.a: Roughly 78% of every donation funds direct programme delivery (training, stipends, equipment, internet). 14% covers Hub operations, and 8% covers organisational overhead. Full breakdown in our annual report — request a copy via email.

registered.a: Yes. Generation Aid is a registered refugee-led organisation operating in the Kakuma camp under host-country regulations, with international fiscal sponsorship for donations.

contact.messageSentBody: We've received your note and a team member will reply within two business days. If your matter is urgent, please call us on the numbers in the sidebar.
contact.nextBody: We'll route your message to the right team.

hero.title: Volunteer with us
hero.subtitle: Lend your skills, time or network to refugee-led change in Kakuma.

hero.title: The organisations walking beside us
hero.subtitle: We're proud to work alongside funders, NGOs and businesses that share our belief in refugee-led change.
hero.subtitleAlt: We work with NGOs, governments and companies that want measurable impact and a direct line to the community they serve.

partners.whyPartnerSubtitle: Four things every partner gets, day one.
partners.currentPartnersSubtitle: A growing network of organisations standing with refugee-led innovation.
partners.threeWaysSubtitle: Every partnership is bespoke — pick the entry point that fits and we'll shape the rest with you.
partners.readyToTalkBody: Send us a short note about your organisation and what you'd like to build together. We respond within two business days.

list.unhcr: Long-standing collaboration on protection, education access and refugee-led programming inside the Kakuma camp.
list.globalgiving: Our primary international donation platform — providing the rails for individual giving and corporate matching campaigns.
list.refugepoint: Implementing partner on durable solutions, livelihoods training and resettlement-ready skills programmes.
list.salesforce: Technology partner — providing the CRM backbone for our beneficiary tracking, alumni engagement and donor stewardship.
list.lwf: Joint delivery of education programming and shared infrastructure across the Kalobeyei integrated settlement.
list.konexio: Pan-African network partner connecting refugee-led organisations with digital-skills curricula, mentorship and cross-border learning exchanges.
list.turkana: Host-community engagement, business permits and joint youth-employment initiatives across Turkana West.

rooted.title: Community-rooted delivery
rooted.body: Every program is designed and run by refugees themselves — meaning higher uptake, lower cost-per-beneficiary and outcomes that stick.

measured.title: Measured impact
measured.body: We track every learner from intake through graduation and into employment. Quarterly reports tied to your funding, no exceptions.

visibility.title: Brand & ESG visibility
visibility.body: Co-branded campaigns, on-the-ground photography and named-room sponsorship in our Digital Community Hub.

talent.title: Talent pipeline
talent.body: Direct hiring access to our Generation Jobs graduates — designers, writers, developers, virtual assistants and customer-support specialists.

programme.name: Programme partner
programme.body: Co-design and co-deliver a specific programme — digital skills, tailoring, English, or remote work — with quarterly impact reports tied to your investment.
features: Two co-branded story features per year
Invitation to the annual partner briefing

hub.name: Hub sponsor
hub.body: Multi-year support for the Digital Community Hub: named-room sponsorship, on-site brand visibility, and a permanent place in our annual report and donor wall.
features: Named room or workstation inside the Hub
Logo on the donor wall + annual report cover
Custom monthly dashboard of usage metrics
Quarterly executive call with our CEO
Site visit hosted by the Generation Aid team

talent.name: Talent partner
talent.body: Hire Generation Jobs graduates directly into remote roles — we handle screening, onboarding and ongoing mentorship at no cost to your company.
features: Optional joint case study after first hire
